_comment: Canonical reference library for the Shivaji series. Accumulates across chapters. 'image' is filled in by tools/refgen.py. visual_description is the locked look used for continuity AND fed into event prompts.
style: Expressive illustrated graphic-novel art style, one consistent look across the whole series: bold confident inked linework, dynamic and cinematic compositions, dramatic lighting with strong light and shadow, rich saturated colour and painterly cel-shading, emotive characterful faces — the look of a high-quality middle-grade graphic novel. Historically grounded in 1630s Maharashtra / the Deccan, India — accurate Maratha clothing, hill-fort architecture, weapons, and landscape. Wholesome and age-appropriate for readers aged 8-12.

jijabai.name: Jijabai
jijabai.visual_description: Jijabai, a dignified Maratha noblewoman with a calm, strong face and steady dark eyes. She wears a traditional nine-yard Maharashtrian saree (nauvari) in deep maroon-red with a green-and-gold border, a gold nose-ring (nath), small gold earrings, and a fine gold-bordered shawl drawn over her head and shoulders. Dark hair parted in the middle and tied back. She wears a small RED, UPWARD-pointing crescent-moon mark (a chandrakor bindi / kunku) at the centre of her forehead. Quiet, upright, brave bearing. (She is in her early thirties and expecting a child only in Chapter 1; in later chapters she is older and NOT pregnant.)
jijabai.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure, calm dignified expression, hands resting together, a small red upward-pointing crescent-moon chandrakor mark on her forehead, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

shahaji.name: Shahaji Bhonsle
shahaji.visual_description: Shahaji Bhonsle, a dignified Maratha warrior-noble of the Deccan in his thirties, matching his documented likeness: a full rounded black beard and moustache, strong features, warm steady eyes. He wears the distinctive tall, pointed Deccan-Maratha turban with a band and an upward-curving feather plume (kalga) at the front, a long robe (jama/angarkha) of rich cloth tied with a sash (kamarband), a pearl necklace, and carries a curved talwar with a disc hilt. Noble, commanding, and kind.
shahaji.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure, one hand resting on his sheathed talwar, the hint of a grey war-horse behind, dignified posture, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

shivaji-baby.name: Shivaji (newborn)
shivaji-baby.visual_description: The newborn Shivaji: a healthy infant with a surprising shock of black hair and small clenched fists, wrapped snugly in a soft cream cloth with a thin saffron border. Bright, alert, full of life.
shivaji-baby.ref_prompt: Reference close study of the swaddled newborn held in cupped hands, plain soft pale background.

killedar-shivneri.name: The killedar of Shivneri
killedar-shivneri.visual_description: The old killedar (fort-keeper) of Shivneri: a lean, grey-bearded Maratha soldier in his sixties with a weathered, kind face and careful eyes. He wears a plain saffron turban, a simple long tunic with a sash, an old shawl, and a short sword. Upright old-soldier bearing.
killedar-shivneri.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure, dignified weathered expression, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

shivneri-fort.name: Shivneri fort
shivneri-fort.visual_description: Shivneri fort: a massive black-basalt hill-fort rising sheer out of morning mist in the Deccan, a green crown of trees at the very top and a pale stone wall running along the clifftop edge. Birds wheel around its cliffs. Vast and ancient, as if the earth itself grew it to guard something precious.
shivneri-fort.ref_prompt: Wide establishing landscape view of the whole hill-fort, no people, soft morning light and mist.

shivneri-gates.name: The seven gates of Shivneri
shivneri-gates.visual_description: The famous fortified gates of Shivneri: a steep climbing stone passage of narrow angled gateways cut into the cliff, heavy wooden doors studded with iron spikes, murder-holes watching from above, each gate turned at a different angle so no charge can run straight. Dark basalt walls, shafts of daylight.
shivneri-gates.ref_prompt: Architectural reference view looking up the steep angled fortified stone gateway passage, no people, dramatic daylight shafts.

shivai-temple.name: Temple of the goddess Shivai
shivai-temple.visual_description: A small, old stone temple tucked among trees near the highest point of Shivneri fort, dedicated to the goddess Shivai. A worn dark-stone shrine with a carved stone idol of the goddess inside, smoothed by a thousand hands, lit by the warm glow of an oil lamp. Peaceful and sacred.
shivai-temple.ref_prompt: Reference view of the small stone temple among trees with a glimpse of the dark stone goddess idol and oil-lamp glow within, no people.

maval-hills.name: The Maval hill-country
maval-hills.visual_description: The Maval country west of Pune: brown-and-green folded hills and valleys rolling away to the horizon like a frozen sea, scattered scrub, terraced fields, and the big open Deccan sky. Beautiful and rugged.
maval-hills.ref_prompt: Wide empty landscape reference of the rolling Deccan hill country, no people, soft natural light.

palki.name: The palki (palanquin)
palki.visual_description: A covered palanquin (palki): a curtained wooden seat slung beneath a single long carrying-pole, with red-and-cream curtains and a simply carved wooden frame, for a noblewoman of the 1630s Deccan.
palki.ref_prompt: Object reference study of the curtained palanquin, three-quarter view, plain soft background, no people.

brass-letter-box.name: Jijabai's brass letter box
brass-letter-box.visual_description: A small hinged brass box, hand-sized, engraved with simple Deccan patterns, holding a few folded letters on rough paper — Shahaji's messages from the war, which Jijabai keeps and rereads.
brass-letter-box.ref_prompt: Object reference study close-up of the small engraved brass box, lid open with folded letters inside, plain soft background.

mavala-soldier.name: Mavala foot-soldier
mavala-soldier.visual_description: A Mavala (Maval hillman) foot-soldier of the western Deccan, the iconic look: a lean, proud warrior with a large upturned curled moustache and a small forehead tilak. SIGNATURE HEADGEAR: a WIDE, FLAT, TWIN-PEAKED (two-cornered) DEEP-MAROON / dark-red Maratha pheta turban worn low — broad and flattish with two soft horns at the sides, NOT tall and NOT pointed and NOT saffron. He wears a WHITE full-sleeved knee-length tunic (angarkha) tied to one side at the chest, a wide bright ORANGE waist sash (kamarband), and WHITE fitted gathered trousers (churidar). FOOTWEAR: enclosed closed-toe traditional Maratha shoes (mojari / juti) with pointed toes that curl sharply UPWARD — NOT open sandals, NOT flat chappals. ARMAMENT: he HOLDS a CURVED talwar sword (curved single-edged blade, disc hilt) in one hand, and on the other arm a SMALL round buckler-style shield — a dark circular metal shield (dhal) bearing FOUR raised bosses / rivets ('buttons') on its front face. Lean and rugged, built for mountain warfare — not heavily armoured.
mavala-soldier.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure facing forward, curved talwar raised in one hand and the small dark four-bossed round buckler on the other arm, plain soft pale background, the whole body and ESPECIALLY the headgear, the upward-pointed enclosed shoes, the curved sword and the small round shield all clearly visible.

shivaji-boy.name: Shivaji (as a boy)
shivaji-boy.visual_description: Young Shivaji at about five years old: a bright, sturdy Maratha boy with a thick mop of glossy black hair, large alert dark eyes, and a curious, fearless, friendly face. He wears a simple everyday outfit of the 1630s Deccan — a plain cream/white short cotton tunic (angi) and a small knee-length dhoti with a little waist sash, often barefoot. Lively and quick, full of questions. (This is the child version of the series' hero; he should read as clearly the same person growing up.)
shivaji-boy.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure of the young boy, bright curious expression, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

deccan-sultanate-soldier.name: Deccan sultanate soldier
deccan-sultanate-soldier.visual_description: A cavalry trooper of one of the Deccan sultanates (Bijapur / Ahmadnagar) — the heavily-equipped 'outside' armies, a deliberate visual contrast to the light white-clad Mavalas. He wears Indo-Persian mail-and-plate armour: a riveted chainmail shirt with large lobed steel chest plates over a bright coloured silk coat, a waist sash, and riding boots. On his head a steel 'top' helmet — a domed helmet with a small spike or plume on top, a sliding nasal bar, and a chainmail aventail hanging to guard the neck. Armed with a curved talwar, a round steel shield, and often a lance or matchlock. Bearded, martial, foreign-looking and well-armoured compared to the lean local hillmen.
deccan-sultanate-soldier.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure in mail-and-plate armour and spiked top helmet, talwar at the hip and round steel shield, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

wooden-sword.name: The wooden practice sword
wooden-sword.visual_description: A child's wooden practice sword, carved by the fort carpenter for young Shivaji — a simple curved talwar shape cut from pale wood, with a plain rounded grip, slightly rough and toy-like but lovingly made. A boy's first sword.
wooden-sword.ref_prompt: Object reference study of the small carved wooden toy sword, three-quarter view, plain soft pale background, no people.

goddess-bhavani.name: The goddess Bhavani
goddess-bhavani.visual_description: The goddess Bhavani (a form of Durga / Devi), as in Jijabai's tales and in Maratha devotion — depicted reverently and warmly. A radiant, powerful Hindu goddess with a serene yet fierce and maternal face, richly adorned in red and gold with fine jewellery and a golden halo of light. She has several arms, each holding a weapon — chiefly a raised curved sword (talwar), plus trident and other arms — and she is accompanied by or rides her tiger. Gentle and protective to the weak, terrible to the cruel. Storybook divine-vision quality, glowing and uplifting, never frightening.
goddess-bhavani.ref_prompt: Reverent reference portrait of the goddess, full figure, glowing divine light, her tiger beside her, simple deep neutral background.

maval-village.name: A Maval hill-village
maval-village.visual_description: A small, humble Maval hill-village of the 1630s Deccan: a cluster of low mud-brick and rough-stone houses with thatched and stone-slab roofs, narrow dusty lanes, a stone well, a few thin cattle, terraced millet fields on the slopes, and a tiny shrine — nestled in the brown-green folds of the Maval hills. Hardworking, ordinary, and vulnerable.
maval-village.ref_prompt: Wide establishing landscape view of the small hill-village among terraced fields in the Maval hills, no people, soft daylight.

first-sword.name: Shahaji's gift — Shivaji's first real sword
first-sword.visual_description: A real but child-sized curved talwar, a father's gift from Shahaji to young Shivaji: a finely made small sword with a gently curved single-edged steel blade and a traditional Maratha disc hilt of dark steel with simple gold inlay, in a red-and-gold scabbard. Beautiful, serious, lovingly chosen — clearly a real weapon scaled down for a boy, not a toy. The grown-up answer to the wooden sword.
first-sword.ref_prompt: Object reference study of the small ornate curved talwar with its red-and-gold scabbard beside it, three-quarter view, plain soft pale background, no people.

dadoji-konddev.name: Dadoji Konddev
dadoji-konddev.visual_description: Dadoji Konddev, the lean, grey, exacting old administrator who managed Shahaji's Pune estate and was young Shivaji's guardian and first teacher. An elderly Maratha gentleman in his sixties with a neat grey moustache and short grey beard, sharp careful eyes, and a calm, no-nonsense, scholarly bearing. He wears dignified traditional administrator's dress of the 1640s Deccan: a clean cream/white angarkha robe tied with a sash, a neatly wound Maratha pheta turban, and a shawl (uparne) over one shoulder. He usually carries a rolled ledger or palm-leaf account scroll, and sometimes wears a sheathed sword (he also oversaw Shivaji's training). Frugal, honest, precise — a teacher and steward, clearly distinct from a soldier.
dadoji-konddev.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure, dignified scholarly-but-stern expression, holding a rolled ledger/account scroll, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

lal-mahal.name: The Lal Mahal (Red Mansion)
lal-mahal.visual_description: The Lal Mahal ('Red Mansion') in Pune, Shivaji's childhood home: a handsome two-storey Maratha mansion of warm red-brown stone and timber, with carved wooden balconies and pillars, arched doorways, an inner courtyard and a shaded veranda, set among the reviving lanes of 1640s Pune with the Deccan hills beyond. Solid, dignified and welcoming — the first real home.
lal-mahal.ref_prompt: Wide establishing view of the warm red-brown two-storey Maratha mansion with its carved wooden balconies and courtyard, no people, warm daylight.

pune-ruined.name: Pune in ruins
pune-ruined.visual_description: The ruined, abandoned town of Pune before its rebuilding: a desolate grey scatter of burnt house-stumps and collapsed mud-and-stone walls with grass and thorn growing through them, choked dry wells, empty lanes, fields gone to scrub, a few thin stray dogs. Hushed, feared, and haunted by war, in the Deccan hills under a bleak sky.
pune-ruined.ref_prompt: Wide establishing view of the desolate ruined abandoned town among the hills, broken and overgrown, no people, soft bleak light.

golden-plough.name: The golden plough (legend)
golden-plough.visual_description: A ceremonial golden plough (sona-cha nangar) from the much-loved Pune legend: shaped like a traditional wooden Indian bullock-plough but worked in gleaming, ornately decorated gold — clearly precious and ceremonial rather than a working tool. Bright, symbolic, and hopeful.
golden-plough.ref_prompt: Object reference study of the ornate golden ceremonial plough, three-quarter view, plain soft pale background, no people.

shivaji-youth.name: Shivaji (as an older boy, ~10-12)
shivaji-youth.visual_description: Shivaji as an older boy of about ten to twelve — the SAME child as the younger reference, now grown: lean, alert and quick, with the same thick black hair and bright intelligent dark eyes and a determined set to his face. Dressed as a young Maratha of the 1640s — a simple cream/white tunic (angi) and dhoti with a waist sash and a small turban or topi, sometimes barefoot or in simple shoes. A capable, watchful boy on the edge of his teens. Must clearly read as the same person as the younger Shivaji, just older.
shivaji-youth.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure of the older boy (around eleven), alert intelligent expression, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

shivaji-teen.name: Shivaji (as a teenager, ~15)
shivaji-teen.visual_description: Shivaji as a young man of about fifteen to seventeen, already looking like his iconic grown-up self rather than a boyish child: a handsome, sharp-featured young Maratha face with an aquiline nose, a THIN moustache and NO beard (clean cheeks and chin), keen confident eyes. He wears Shivaji's iconic light-coloured wound turban (pheta/jiretop) in this DISTINCTIVE SHAPE: the turban sits LOW and rounded across the forehead and brows at the FRONT, then sweeps UP and BACKWARD to a soft raised PEAK at the upper BACK of the head — it is NOT pointed at the front and NOT a symmetric round dome; the high point and bulk are toward the BACK. A thick ornamental TASSEL — a hanging bunch of cords and pearls — dangles DOWN from that rear peak, behind and beside his ear. The cloth is light cream/white with a fine woven, lightly quilted (cross-hatch) texture and a subtle gold edge; NOT a solid-gold metal cap or helmet. He also wears a large gold hoop earring, a fine white/cream angarkha tunic with a patterned sash (patka) worn diagonally across the chest, CLOSE-FITTING white churidar trousers (snug to the legs, like the Mavala foot-soldiers wear — NOT a loose baggy dhoti), simple shoes, and a curved talwar at his side. Charismatic, dignified, a born leader and king-in-the-making — the recognizable young-Shivaji look.
shivaji-teen.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure of the roughly sixteen-year-old, regal confident bearing, wearing the white wound turban with its rear peak and side tassel exactly like the source photo, one hand near his talwar. NATURAL, REALISTIC BODY PROPORTIONS — the head sized appropriately for the body, NOT oversized. He carries only a sheathed talwar — NO shield. Plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

shivaji-young-man.name: Shivaji (as a young adult, ~18-20, full beard)
shivaji-young-man.visual_description: Shivaji as a young adult man of about eighteen to twenty — the SAME person as the earlier teenage reference, now matured into a full adult, still clearly recognizable as the same individual: a handsome, sharp-featured Maratha man with an aquiline nose and keen, intelligent, confident eyes. STURDY, STRONG, BROAD-SHOULDERED full-adult warrior's build (solid and powerful, NOT a lean slight boy). FACIAL HAIR — the iconic look: a FULL, neatly-kept THICK BLACK BEARD framing the jaw and chin, with a BOLD, THICK, slightly UPTURNED black MOUSTACHE (fuller and more commanding than a thin youth's beard; not bushy or wild — groomed and regal). HEADGEAR — Shivaji's iconic light cream/white wound turban (pheta / jiretop) in his DISTINCTIVE ESTABLISHED shape, EXACTLY like the earlier younger-Shivaji references: it sits LOW and rounded across the forehead and brows at the FRONT, then sweeps UP and BACKWARD to a soft raised PEAK at the upper BACK of the head — it is NOT pointed at the front and NOT a symmetric round dome; the height and bulk are toward the BACK. A thick ornamental TASSEL (a hanging bunch of cords and pearls) dangles straight DOWN from that rear peak, behind and beside one ear. The cloth is light cream/white with a fine quilted (cross-hatch) texture and a subtle gold edge — NOT a solid-gold cap or helmet. A small RED, UPWARD-pointing crescent-moon mark (chandrakor tilak) at the centre of his forehead. JEWELLERY — he wears SEVERAL STRANDS OF WHITE PEARL NECKLACES around his neck (a signature feature) together with a fine gold necklace, large gold hoop earrings, and gem rings on his fingers. DRESS — a fine white/cream angarkha tunic with rich gold borders and a patterned silk sash (patka) worn diagonally across the chest, close-fitting white churidar trousers, and a curved talwar with a disc hilt at his side. Charismatic, dignified, regal — a warrior-king in the making. NATURAL, REALISTIC body and head proportions.
shivaji-young-man.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure of the sturdy ~19-year-old, regal confident bearing; his iconic established cream/white wound jiretop turban (low at the brow, sweeping up to a soft PEAK at the BACK with the tassel hanging DOWN at the back — matching the earlier younger Shivaji references); a full thick black beard and bold upturned moustache; several strands of pearl necklaces; a small red upward-crescent chandrakor on the forehead; one hand near his sheathed talwar (NO shield); natural realistic proportions; plain soft pale background.

tanaji-malusare.name: Tanaji Malusare
tanaji-malusare.visual_description: Tanaji Malusare, Shivaji's closest and most famous Maval companion: a powerfully built, broad-shouldered young hill-warrior with a strong jaw and a bold thick moustache, and a warm, open, expressive face — quick to grin in good times, but equally capable of deep solemnity when the moment calls for it (his expression should always suit the scene, NOT default to grinning). Warm, blunt, fearless and utterly devoted. Dressed in the everyday Maval style — a white/cream tunic and dhoti with a bold red sash and the signature WIDE, FLAT, TWIN-PEAKED deep-maroon quilted Mavala pheta turban (worn low and broad with two soft corners, exactly like the Mavala foot-soldier's headgear in the reference image — NOT tall, NOT pointed), a curved talwar at his waist. A larger-than-life, friendly, strong presence — the muscle and the warm heart of the band.
tanaji-malusare.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure, powerful build, broad cheerful grin, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

yesaji-kank.name: Yesaji Kank
yesaji-kank.visual_description: Yesaji Kank, a Maval companion of Shivaji: a wiry, quick, quietly intense young hill-fighter and superb swordsman — lean and watchful, with level brows, sharp eyes and a short moustache. Dressed in everyday Maval style — white/cream tunic and dhoti, a red sash, and the signature WIDE, FLAT, TWIN-PEAKED deep-maroon quilted Mavala pheta turban (worn low and broad with two soft corners, exactly like the Mavala foot-soldier's headgear in the reference image — NOT tall, NOT pointed) — with a fine curved talwar he plainly knows how to use. Calm, alert and deadly-capable: the quiet, skilled one of the band.
yesaji-kank.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure, alert watchful stance, one hand resting on his sword hilt, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

baji-pasalkar.name: Baji Pasalkar
baji-pasalkar.visual_description: Baji Pasalkar, an older Maval deshmukh (village chief) and one of Shivaji's first senior supporters: a dignified, weathered man in his fifties with grey coming into his thick moustache and hair, a strong steady face and a leader's calm authority. Dressed a touch more substantially than the youths — a clean white angarkha robe with a good sash, a well-wound maroon/saffron Maratha pheta turban, a shawl over one shoulder, and a curved talwar. The respected senior man whose loyalty lends the young band weight.
baji-pasalkar.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing dignified figure, steady authoritative expression, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

adil-shah.name: The Sultan of Bijapur (Mohammed Adil Shah)
adil-shah.visual_description: Mohammed Adil Shah, Sultan of Bijapur (the Adilshahi ruler) — depicted with DIGNITY as a refined Deccan sultan, never a caricature. A plump, composed man with a neat dark beard and calm heavy-lidded eyes, and a faint golden halo (nimbus) behind his head in the Deccani portrait tradition. He wears opulent 17th-century Deccani court finery: a fine flowing white/cream muslin jama (robe) over a jewelled sash (patka), strings of large pearls and emeralds, gold armbands and rings, and a jewelled turban topped with a sarpech (turban-jewel) and an aigrette plume. He often holds a single flower (a floral sprig), a sign of cultured refinement. Wealthy, serene and grand — the embodiment of sultanate splendour.
adil-shah.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing dignified figure in opulent Deccani court robes holding a single flower, a faint golden halo behind the head, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

bijapur-court.name: The Bijapur durbar (Adilshahi court)
bijapur-court.visual_description: The durbar (great audience hall) of the Adilshahi court at Bijapur: a vast, opulent Deccan-Islamic hall with soaring scalloped arches, a great dome, intricate tilework and frescoes, rich patterned carpets, hanging lamps and a haze of incense, a playing fountain, and at the far end a high canopied jewelled throne on a raised dais. Splendid, perfumed and ceremonial — the seat of immense sultanate wealth.
bijapur-court.ref_prompt: Wide establishing interior view of the opulent domed Deccan-Islamic royal audience hall — soaring arches, rich carpets, hanging lamps, and a high canopied throne at the far end, no people, rich warm light.

raireshwar-temple.name: The Raireshwar temple
raireshwar-temple.visual_description: The ancient Raireshwar Shiva temple, high on its windswept Sahyadri plateau: a small, weathered, plain dark-stone temple in the old Hemadpanthi style — strong square stone pillars, simple carvings, a modest stone shikhara (tower) — standing alone under a vast mountain sky. Inside, in the dark, sits a simple stone Shiva lingam on a low altar, lit by a single small oil lamp; no gold and no ornament — just ancient stone and a small flame. Austere, sacred, timeless — the deliberate opposite of a golden sultanate court.
raireshwar-temple.ref_prompt: Reference view of the small ancient stone Shiva temple interior — square Hemadpanthi pillars and a simple stone Shiva lingam lit by a single oil lamp in the dark — with a glimpse of the high plateau and mountain sky beyond the doorway, no people.

torna-fort.name: Torna fort (Prachandagad)
torna-fort.visual_description: Torna fort, also called Prachandagad ('the huge, fierce fort') — the highest hill-fort in the Pune country of the Sahyadri mountains. A vast, steep, tilted mountain of grey basalt rock, its long ridges crowned with rugged dark-stone defensive ramparts and round bastions that snake for a great distance along the clifftops, with a few heavy arched stone gateways (darwaza) set into the climb and a broad flat plateau-top (machi) bearing a small old stone shrine. Sheer cliffs fall away on every side; clouds catch and tear on its summit in the wet season. Immense, ancient and forbidding — a giant grey stronghold dwarfing the rolling green-brown Maval valleys far below.
torna-fort.ref_prompt: Wide establishing landscape view of the whole towering grey hill-fort and its long ridge-top ramparts seen across a deep valley, no people, soft natural daylight with balanced colour.

rajgad-fort.name: Rajgad fort (the King of Forts)
rajgad-fort.visual_description: Rajgad, 'the King of Forts' — Shivaji's own great new stronghold and long-time capital, built up from the hill once called Murumbdev in the Sahyadri mountains. A vast, sprawling grey-basalt mountain fortress: a high, rocky central citadel (the balekilla) crowning the summit, with long rugged stone rampart-walls, bastions and battlements radiating outward and snaking along three broad plateau-spurs (machis) that reach out from the central peak like the arms of a star. Strong arched stone gateways, watchtowers, cisterns and storehouses on the broad plateaus. Cliffs fall away on all sides; rolling green-brown Maval ranges stretch to the horizon. Grander, stronger and more deliberately engineered than old neglected Torna — a fort built new and whole, clearly cared for. Mighty and proud.
rajgad-fort.ref_prompt: Wide establishing aerial-ish landscape view of the whole great sprawling hill-fort — a high central citadel with long fortified rampart-walls radiating along several mountain spurs — seen from across the ranges, no people, soft natural daylight with balanced colour.

treasure-hoard.name: The buried treasure hoard
treasure-hoard.visual_description: An ancient buried treasure hoard, just dug from the earth: two or three fat round clay pots, their old wax-sealed mouths cracked open, spilling hundreds of small thick old gold coins (hons) that gleam warmly in the light. Crumbling dark soil clings to the pots; loose gold coins scatter on the freshly turned ground around them. The forgotten pay-chest of some long-ago war — humble earthenware full of astonishing wealth.
treasure-hoard.ref_prompt: Object reference study of the broken clay treasure pots spilling old gold coins onto dark dug earth, three-quarter view, plain soft background, no people.

kondhana-fort.name: Kondhana fort (later Sinhagad)
kondhana-fort.visual_description: Kondhana, a mighty and well-kept hill-fort of the Sahyadri mountains southwest of Pune (later renamed Sinhagad). A high three-pronged flat-topped plateau ringed by sheer black-basalt cliffs that rise tens of metres to strong, continuous grey-stone ramparts studded with regularly spaced square watchtowers and bastions. Two great fortified gateways (darwaza) of thick iron-banded wooden doors set in twisting, dog-legged stone passages guard the approaches. Broad green grassy plateau on top with stone cisterns and old buildings. Unlike neglected Torna, this fort is strong, intact and formidable — a place you could never storm cheaply. Green and misty in the monsoon, commanding immense views of the ranges.
kondhana-fort.ref_prompt: Wide establishing landscape view of the whole great flat-topped cliff-girt hill-fort and its long ramparts and towers seen across the ranges, no people, soft natural daylight with balanced colour.

purandar-fort.name: Purandar fort
purandar-fort.visual_description: Purandar, a mighty double-crowned twin-fort of the Sahyadri mountains southeast of Pune. It rises in two great storeys: a high upper citadel (the balekilla) crowning the summit, standing above a broad lower walled terrace-plateau (the machi) that is reinforced with several large round bastions and an imposing main gateway flanked by two towers. A smaller separate sister-fort, Vajragad, stands guard on a neighbouring spur to the east, linked by a saddle. Sheer grey-and-ochre basalt slopes and tiered stone ramparts; sweeping panoramic views over the surrounding country. Strong, layered and proud — a fortress in two levels.
purandar-fort.ref_prompt: Wide establishing landscape view of the great two-tiered twin hill-fort — high upper citadel above a broad bastioned lower terrace, with the smaller sister-fort on a neighbouring spur — seen across the ranges, no people, soft natural daylight with balanced colour.

siddi-amber.name: Siddi Amber (killedar of Kondhana)
siddi-amber.visual_description: Siddi Amber, the elderly Adilshahi (Bijapur) commander and killedar of Kondhana fort — a Habshi (Siddi), a Deccan soldier of African descent, depicted with full dignity and gravitas, never a caricature. An older man of perhaps sixty with dark brown skin, a short grizzled grey beard and moustache, a weathered, intelligent, dryly humorous face and the straight back of a lifelong professional soldier. He wears the dress of a senior 17th-century Deccan sultanate officer: a good-quality long coat or jama tied with a sash (kamarband), a neatly wound turban, a shawl, and a sheathed curved talwar at his side. Calm, shrewd, war-weary and self-possessed — a reasonable veteran, not a villain.
siddi-amber.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing dignified figure of the elderly Siddi commander, calm shrewd weathered expression, one hand resting near his sheathed talwar, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

rajmudra-seal.name: The Rajmudra (Shivaji's royal seal)
rajmudra-seal.visual_description: Shivaji's royal seal — the authentic Rajmudra: a single octagonal (eight-sided) seal emblem bearing the real Sanskrit royal inscription in clear Devanagari script, the lettering arranged in horizontal lines within a bold octagonal border. This is the genuine historical seal EXACTLY as in the provided source image — its octagonal shape, line layout and every Devanagari character preserved faithfully and legibly. A dignified emblem of a ruler's authority.
rajmudra-seal.ref_prompt: Faithful flat front-on rendering of the whole octagonal Rajmudra seal emblem with its Devanagari inscription, centred, on a plain neutral/dark background, no people — preserve the source's text and form exactly.

fateh-khan.name: Fateh Khan (Bijapur general)
fateh-khan.visual_description: Fateh Khan, the capable Bijapur (Adilshahi) general sent in 1648 to crush Shivaji — an individual antagonist depicted with martial dignity and gravitas, NOT a caricature and never a stand-in for a people or faith. A confident, hard-faced commander in his forties with a strong dark beard and moustache and a stern, proud expression. He wears grand senior commander's war-gear of the 17th-century Deccan: fine Indo-Persian mail-and-plate armour with lobed steel chest plates over a rich coloured silk coat, gold-trimmed, a sash, and a fine domed steel helmet with a small plume/jewel and a chainmail neck-guard (or a richly wound battle turban). He carries a curved talwar and a round steel shield, and rides a strong, well-caparisoned war-horse. Grander, richer and more individual than the ordinary heavy sultanate trooper — clearly a high commander.
fateh-khan.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length figure of the proud middle-aged general in fine mail-and-plate armour and plumed helmet, curved talwar at his side, a strong war-horse beside or behind him, dignified martial bearing, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

murad-bakhsh.name: Prince Murad Bakhsh (Mughal prince)
murad-bakhsh.visual_description: Prince Murad Bakhsh, a son of the Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan and the Mughal viceroy of the Deccan around 1649 — depicted with full dignity as a young Mughal royal, an individual, never a caricature. A young man of about twenty-five with fair skin, fine refined features, and a neat short dark beard and moustache. He wears opulent NORTHERN-MUGHAL court finery (distinct from the Deccan-Bijapuri style): a fine flowing white/ivory or richly-coloured muslin jama (robe) tied with a jewelled patka sash, ropes of large pearls and gemstones, gold armbands and rings, and a Mughal turban topped with a jewelled turban-ornament (sarpech) and an upright feather aigrette (kalgi). A faint golden halo (nimbus) behind his head in the Mughal portrait tradition. Holds himself with the assured grace of imperial power. Wealthy, cultured, powerful.
murad-bakhsh.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing dignified young Mughal prince in opulent Mughal court robes and a jewelled feathered turban, a faint golden halo behind the head, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

mughal-court.name: The Mughal court (Deccan)
mughal-court.visual_description: A grand Mughal audience hall — the opulent court of a Mughal prince, in the rich NORTHERN-Mughal imperial style (distinct from the Deccan-Islamic Bijapur durbar): elegant cusped (multifoil) arches in pale red-sandstone and white marble, fine pietra-dura floral inlay, slender columns, sumptuous carpets and bolsters, hanging lamps, gauzy drapes, and a raised canopied throne/dais. Airy, immense, magnificent — the seat of the mightiest empire in the world. Cool, refined imperial grandeur.
mughal-court.ref_prompt: Wide establishing interior view of the opulent white-marble-and-red-sandstone Mughal audience hall with cusped arches, inlay and a canopied throne, no people, refined imperial light.

bahirji-naik.name: Bahirji Naik (spymaster)
bahirji-naik.visual_description: Bahirji Naik, Shivaji's spymaster and head of his scout/intelligence network — deliberately ORDINARY and forgettable, a man built to vanish in any crowd. A lean, wiry Maratha man in his forties of average height, with a plain, unremarkable, weathered face and SHARP, watchful, intelligent eyes (the only striking thing about him). He dresses as a humble traveller / wandering performer: a simple modest off-white tunic and dhoti, a worn wrap or shawl over one shoulder, and a small plain head-cloth or modest turban — NOT rich, NOT a warrior-noble's finery. He carries a wanderer's props that double as disguise: a walking staff, a cloth shoulder-bundle, perhaps a small hand-drum. Quiet, observant, clever and self-effacing — a master of disguise who could pass as a singer, a beggar, a trader or a pilgrim.
bahirji-naik.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure of the lean, ordinary, watchful spy in humble traveller's clothes with a walking staff and shoulder-bundle, modest and unremarkable, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.

javli-forest.name: Javli (the forest kingdom)
javli-forest.visual_description: Javli — a wild, almost impassable forested mountain realm on the western edge of the Sahyadris, tilting down toward the distant Konkan coast. A vast tangle of densely wooded hills and deep shadowed ravines, with rushing streams and steep gorges, and a forest canopy grown so thick that little sunlight reaches the leaf-littered floor. Drifting mist between the trees, twisted roots, dim green half-light, a brooding secretive depth. It is a natural fortress of trees rather than stone — a green, drowned, dangerous jungle-kingdom that swallows strangers and hides its keepers. Layered blue-green ridges receding into haze.
javli-forest.ref_prompt: Wide establishing landscape of the dense dark Sahyadri forest realm — deeply wooded folded hills and shadowed ravines, a rushing stream, drifting mist, dim green light under a thick canopy, no people, brooding and secretive mood, balanced natural colour and daylight (avoid an artificial yellow wash).

chandrarao-more.name: Chandrarao More (chief of Javli)
chandrarao-more.visual_description: Chandrarao More, the proud hereditary Maratha chief of the forest country of Javli — depicted as a dignified individual antagonist driven by pride and politics, NOT a caricature and never a stand-in for any people or faith (he is himself a Maratha hill-lord, the same people as Shivaji). A haughty, confident Maratha clan-lord in his late forties to fifties, weathered and strong-jawed, with a bold thick upturned grey-flecked black moustache and a short beard, and a proud, faintly contemptuous, imperious expression. He wears the rich dress of a senior Maratha forest-chieftain (deshmukh-class): a fine deep-coloured angarkha robe with gold borders, a patterned sash (patka), a shawl, gold necklaces and rings, and a Maratha pheta turban (deep maroon/saffron) — distinctly MARATHA, NOT sultanate Indo-Persian armour. A curved talwar with a disc hilt at his side. Lordly, arrogant, self-assured bearing — a man used to bowing to no one.
chandrarao-more.ref_prompt: Character reference sheet: full-length standing figure of the proud middle-aged Maratha forest-chieftain in a rich gold-bordered angarkha, sash, shawl and maroon Maratha pheta, bold upturned moustache and short beard, gold necklaces, a curved talwar at his side, haughty self-assured bearing, plain soft pale background, full body clearly visible.
